title: SEGMENT 15 — Phages in food safety: already in your supermarket

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Phages in food safety: already in your supermarket

Now let me bring this down from the theoretical to the very practical, because phages are already in commercial use in a way that most people don't know about, and the example is worth knowing because it shows the regulatory path can work.
In 2006, the FDA approved a phage product called ListShield — manufactured by Intralytix — for use as a food spray against Listeria monocytogenes on ready-to-eat meat and poultry products.
Deli meats.
Cold cuts.
Hot dogs.
The product is a cocktail of six phages, all lytic, all specific to Listeria, sprayed on the surface of processed meats before packaging.

The phages kill any Listeria that might be on the surface, reducing the risk of listeriosis — a foodborne illness that's particularly dangerous for pregnant women, the elderly, and the immunocompromised.
The product is GRAS — Generally Recognized As Safe — and is approved for use on organic foods, on kosher foods, on a wide range of products.
It is already in your supermarket.
If you've eaten any commercial deli meat in the last fifteen years, there's a reasonable chance you've consumed bacteriophages with it.
The analogy I want you to hold for this segment: FDA-approved Listeria-killing phage spray on deli meats.
Already in your supermarket.
Already in you, in trace quantities, if you eat processed meat.

The regulatory acceptance came through the food safety pathway, which is different from the drug pathway — phages applied to food are regulated as food additives, not as drugs, and the bar is somewhat lower.
But the precedent matters.
The FDA has formally accepted that phage applications to surfaces in contact with humans are safe, that they work, that they reduce pathogen loads.
That precedent has greased the path for broader phage approvals.
Other food safety phage products have followed.
EcoShield for E. coli O157:H7 on beef.
SalmoFresh for Salmonella on poultry.
ShigaShield for Shigella on produce.
The food safety market for phages is small but growing, and it's been a quiet proof-of-concept for the regulatory and manufacturing challenges of getting phage products into the marketplace.
There are also agricultural applications — phages applied to crops to prevent bacterial plant diseases.
AgriPhage for Xanthomonas on tomatoes and peppers.
Phage products for fire blight on apples.
Aquaculture applications — phages for fish farm pathogens like Aeromonas and Vibrio.
The list is small but growing.

The broader point: phage technology has been quietly entering commercial agriculture and food production for two decades, building a track record of safety and efficacy that may eventually grease the path for the therapeutic applications.
The regulatory and manufacturing infrastructure is partly already in place, just for different applications.
That existing infrastructure is one of the reasons the therapy resurgence is plausible — we're not starting from zero.
We've been making and selling phage products for a long time.
